explicit_text: I moved to the University of California, Berkeley in 1937 to begin my graduate studies and remained there for much of my career. Berkeley was a dynamic place, especially at the Radiation Laboratory under Ernest Lawrence’s leadership. I eventually served as chairman of the Physics Department from 1955 to 1962, navigating challenges like faculty hiring, the loyalty oath controversy, and curriculum development. Berkeley was central to my research and academic leadership, and I remained engaged with the university community even after retirement.

The cyclotron technology was central to much of the experimental work I was involved in throughout my career.

explicit_text: In 1964, I was invited to be one of ten game theorists advising the United States Arms Control and Disarmament Agency on negotiations with the Soviet Union. The other advisers felt they could not help due to the lack of information on both sides, but I developed a method to assign probabilities to possible moves and outcomes. This work was a practical application of my game theory research and had implications for international negotiations.

explicit_text: I remember the 1948 West Coast waterfront strike vividly because my father was a longshoreman working nights during that time. explicit_text: I came onto the waterfront in Portland, Oregon in 1929 during the Depression. We lived in a small four-room house, and work was scarce, so I took any job I could get, even if it paid very little. Portland was where I first got involved with waterfront work and learned about union struggles from the older men in the gangs. Later, in 1931, we started reorganizing the union membership in Portland, though it was difficult to hold onto it until the Wagner Act was passed.
implicit_text: I came onto the waterfront in the city known for its bridges and roses in 1929 during the Depression. We lived in a small four-room house, and work was scarce, so I took any job I could get, even if it paid very little. That city was where I first got involved with waterfront work and learned about union struggles from the older men in the gangs. Later, in 1931, we started reorganizing the union membership there, though it was difficult to hold onto it until the Wagner Act was passed.

explicit_text: Ory Cole was an old friend I ran into on the Portland waterfront when I first started in 1929. He was working in a steady gang and helped me get a job filling in with the Pete Hendrickson gang. Ory was part of the group of older men who shared their experiences about past strikes and union struggles, which shaped my understanding and determination to help build a union.

entity_aliases: Wall Street Journal|WSJ|The Journal|Wall Street journal (Eastern ed.)|The Wall Street journal. Eastern edition
entity_description_wiki: The Wall Street Journal (WSJ), commonly known as the Journal, is an American newspaper based in Midtown Manhattan, New York City.
explicit_text: The Wall Street Journal published editorials praising Polish workers for their right to strike, yet the very next day it denounced the Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization for striking in the U.S. This hypocrisy was typical of the media's stance on unions, supporting them abroad but opposing them at home. It was pitiful to see such double standards while union membership in the U.S. was at an all-time low.

It was pitiful to see such double standards while union membership in the U.S. was at an all-time low.

entity_description: 1935 U.S. federal labor law regulating the rights of workers and unions
explicit_text: Before the Wagner Act was passed, it was very hard to hold union membership because employers controlled hiring and actively tried to eliminate union men. After the Wagner Act came into effect, it provided legal protections that helped us maintain union membership and organize more effectively. The Act was a turning point in labor history, giving workers more rights and protections on the waterfront and beyond.

entity_description_wiki: The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) is an African-American civil rights organization in the United States that played a pivotal role for African Americans in the civil rights movement.
explicit_text: The Congress of Racial Equality, or CORE, was one of the activist groups that joined forces with the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and SNCC in the civil rights movement. CORE's involvement helped broaden the scope and impact of our nonviolent protests during the early 1960s.

entity_description_wiki: Yolanda Denise King (November 17, 1955 – May 15, 2007) was an American activist and campaigner for African-American rights and first-born child of civil rights leaders Martin Luther King Jr.
explicit_text: Yolanda King is my oldest child, and even at six years old, she was aware of segregation and the civil rights struggle. I remember explaining to her why she couldn't go to Fun Town, a local amusement park, because it was closed to colored children. It was painful to see her tears and realize the first dark cloud of inferiority had touched her, but I reassured her that many white people wanted integration. Later, when Fun Town quietly desegregated, I took her there, and she heard white visitors express happiness to see us.

explicit_text: I attended the University of Texas at Austin after two years of junior college, starting in the late 1950s. I pursued my master's in English there and became a national student Y officer, which deepened my engagement with race and peace issues. It was also at UT Austin that I met Connie Curry and got involved with the sit-in movement and southern student organizing.

entity_aliases: Freedom Summer of 1964|Mississippi Freedom Project|Mississippi Summer Project
explicit_text: The Mississippi Summer Project was a major civil rights campaign in which I participated through SNCC starting in the early 1960s. The project was dangerous but vital, and it brought together activists from across the country to challenge segregation and disenfranchisement in Mississippi.

entity_description_wiki: Courts of Mississippi include:
explicit_text: The Circuit Court of Mississippi played a major role in suppressing Black voters by arbitrarily removing many names from voter rolls in 1956. Medgar Evers and others worked to help people re-register despite the court's discriminatory practices, including impossible literacy tests. We often had to send people alone to the court to avoid retaliation, as the circuit clerk was hostile to Black voters and activists.

explicit_text: Dr. Kenneth Clark, a Negro professor of psychology at the City College of New York, made some insightful observations comparing Martin Luther King Jr. and the Black Muslims. He suggested that King's philosophy, while appearing healthy and stable, might place an unrealistic psychological burden on victims of oppression by asking them to love their oppressors. I found his views thought-provoking, especially as they relate to the natural human response to injustice and bitterness.

I found his views thought-provoking, especially as they relate to the natural human response to injustice and bitterness.

entity_description_wiki: John Dollard (29 August 1900 – 8 October 1980) was an American psychologist and social scientist known for his studies on race relations in America and the frustration-aggression hypothesis he proposed with Neal E. Miller and others.
explicit_text: John Dollard's work, especially his book 'Caste and Class in a Southern Town,' helped me understand the psychological effects of oppression on Negroes. His analysis of aggression being turned inward or against others resonated with my own experiences during demonstrations and encounters with racial barriers. His research supports the idea that nonviolence, despite its challenges, is a necessary path.

explicit_text: On February 1, 1960, I was in my hometown, Greensboro, North Carolina, when I heard on the radio about four students from A&T college sitting at the Woolworth's lunch counter demanding service. Greensboro was also the place where my cousin's ex was murdered by Ku Klux Klan bullets years later, marking it as a site of both historic protest and personal tragedy for me.

entity_description_wiki: The Imperial New Delhi is a historic luxury hotel opened in 1936 in New Delhi, India.
explicit_text: The Imperial Hotel in Delhi was the venue for a critical All-India Muslim League meeting on June 9, 1947, where the truncated Pakistan was accepted. Its significance lies in being a backdrop to these historic and tragic moments.

entity_aliases: Tule Lake War Relocation Center|Tule Lake Relocation Center|TULE
entity_description_wiki: The Tule Lake War Relocation Center, also known as the Tule Lake Segregation Center, was an American concentration camp located in Modoc and Siskiyou counties in California and constructed in 1942 by the United States government to incarcerate Japanese Americans, forcibly removing them from their homes on the West Coast of the United States.
explicit_text: I was sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center when I was about 19 years old, right after the war started. Tule Lake became a segregation camp when the so-called no-no boys and pro-Japanese people were sent there. My mother refused to leave Tule Lake because she wanted to go back home, but the War Relocation Authority had not allowed families to return yet. I lived in Block 45, and the camp was huge with many wards and blocks, each with its own facilities. Life there was hard, but we made friends and tried to keep to ourselves despite the tensions caused by the no-no boys.
implicit_text: I was sent to a segregation camp in northern California when I was about 19 years old, right after the war started. This camp became a segregation center when the so-called no-no boys and pro-Japanese people were sent there. My mother refused to leave because she wanted to go back home, but the government agency in charge had not allowed families to return yet. I lived in one of the blocks, and the camp was huge with many wards and blocks, each with its own facilities. Life there was hard, but we made friends and tried to keep to ourselves despite the tensions caused by the no-no boys.

explicit_text: Alameda is where Earl Warren once lived, a place connected to the man who played a role in the discrimination against Japanese Americans. entity_aliases: Executive Order of February 19, 1942|Executive Order No. 9066|EO 9066|United States Executive Order 9066
entity_description: 1942 U.S. presidential order for the internment of Japanese-Americans
entity_description_wiki: Executive Order 9066 was a United States presidential executive order signed and issued during World War II by United States president Franklin D. Roosevelt on February 19, 1942.
explicit_text: Executive Order 9066 was the government order that led to our forced removal from our homes and placement in camps like Tule Lake. Signed by President Roosevelt after Pearl Harbor, it gave the military authority to exclude people from certain areas. This order uprooted our lives and questioned our loyalty, even though we were American citizens. It was the legal basis for the injustice we suffered during the war.

entity_description: Christian commemoration of the resurrection of Jesus
entity_description_wiki: Easter, also called Pasch () or Pascha or Resurrection Sunday, is a Christian festival and cultural holiday commemorating the resurrection of Jesus from the dead, described in the Bible's New Testament as having occurred on the third day of his burial following his crucifixion by the Romans at Calvary c. 30 AD. It is the culmination of the Passion of Jesus, preceded by Lent (or Great Lent), a 40-day period of fasting, prayer, and penance.
explicit_text: Easter 2020 was unlike any other because it happened during the early weeks of the COVID-19 pandemic. At Notre Dame’s Basilica of the Sacred Heart, we suspended in-person masses and held services with only a small group of Holy Cross religious present, spaced six feet apart. We livestreamed the liturgies, which was a major shift from our usual communal celebrations.
implicit_text: The spring religious celebration in 2020 was unlike any other because it happened during the early weeks of the pandemic. We livestreamed the liturgies, which was a major shift from our usual communal celebrations.

explicit_text: I was born in St. Augustine but raised in New York. In 1964, I returned to St. Augustine to participate in the civil rights Movement, joining sit-ins and protests. Although I faced hostility and fear among locals, my involvement helped push the Movement forward in that city.

entity_aliases: Halifax County, North Carolina|Halifax County, NC
entity_description: county in North Carolina, United States
entity_description_wiki: Halifax County is a county located in the U.S. state of North Carolina.
explicit_text: Halifax County was where we started our North Carolina Black Belt campaign west of Bertie, facing fierce resistance from groups like the Klan and the John Birch Society. It was like trench warfare, with violence and economic reprisals against activists. Despite this, we held meetings and demonstrations that culminated in a sweeping voter registration victory in Federal Court in Raleigh. The success in Halifax spread hope and momentum to other counties in the Black Belt.

The State Press was more than a business; it was a tool for advocacy and change.

explicit_text: I was attending school in Tucson, Arizona when I decided to join the Freedom Rides. It was from there that I answered Jim Farmer's call to take part in the civil rights movement. Tucson was my starting point before heading into the Deep South for the rides.

explicit_text: Hank Thomas was one of the Freedom Riders on the bus in Anniston, Alabama. When the gas tank exploded, he bravely forced open the bus door, allowing us to escape the fire. His quick action saved lives during that terrifying attack.

explicit_text: United Airlines was the airline we tried to use to get from Birmingham to Montgomery during the Freedom Rides. There was a bomb threat against our flight, which led to a federal search of the plane. With the Assistant Attorney General on board to prove there was no bomb, we were finally allowed to fly safely.

entity_description_wiki: The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) is an African-American civil rights organization in the United States that played a pivotal role for African Americans in the civil rights movement.

entity_description_wiki: Baptists are a Protestant tradition of Christianity distinguished by baptizing only believers (believer's baptism) and doing so by total immersion.
explicit_text: I became a church member at the age of nine in the good old Baptist tradition. My grandfather on my mother's side was a minister with several churches in Warren County. The academy where my parents met may have had ties to New England Baptists, and my father's brother was prominent in Cherry Street Baptist Church in Philadelphia. The Baptist faith was a strong part of my family's life and heritage.

entity_description: race based restrictions in American society
entity_description_wiki: Facilities and services such as housing, healthcare, education, employment, and transportation have been systematically separated in the United States based on racial categorizations.
explicit_text: I grew up during segregation in the United States, specifically in North Carolina, where the schools and communities were separated by race. This was the norm in my early years, and it shaped much of my daily life and interactions. Segregation was something you didn’t always think about until you encountered white people or crossed those boundaries. It wasn’t until later, as I got older and went to high school, that I started to reflect more on what segregation really meant and how it affected us all.

I went straight to the SNCC office after changing clothes, and James Daniels was already bragging about what we did. explicit_text: I trained at the Great Lakes Naval Training Center, where my company was the last all-black company in the Navy. After basic training there, I was assigned to the Seventh Fleet and sent to the Pacific theater.

entity_description: village in Dunn County, Wisconsin, United States
entity_description_wiki: Boyceville is a village in Dunn County, Wisconsin, United States.
explicit_text: I came to Boyceville in August 2002 and have been part of this small town ever since. Boyceville is a tight-knit community in Northwest Wisconsin with just over a thousand people, where everyone tends to know each other’s histories. It’s still largely a farming town, but many residents commute to Menomonie, Eau Claire, or even Minneapolis–Saint Paul for work. As a pastor here, I’m deeply involved in the community, not just through church but also by participating in local activities like announcing sports games and serving as a crossing guard. The town’s strong sense of connection and support has made it a special place to live and serve.

It is the culmination of the Passion of Jesus, preceded by Lent (or Great Lent), a 40-day period of fasting, prayer, and penance.

explicit_text: I live in Ottawa with my partner and our three cats. Ottawa has been both the backdrop and the setting for many of the challenges and changes in my life during these years.

explicit_text: Cabbage is the main crop that most squatters, including my family, try to grow near the swamp lands of Florida. Growing cabbage is a key part of our subsistence farming, even if it rarely goes beyond small garden patches.

explicit_text: I started out listening to Joe Oliver play at the Dreamland Cafe and the Peek-In Cafe when I was just a kid. Joe's music and style deeply influenced me during my early years in Chicago.

Though we were considered Yankees in Florida, our roots in that Michigan city were strong and part of our family identity.

entity_description: North American federation of labor unions from 1935 to 1955
entity_description_wiki: The Congress of Industrial Organizations (CIO) was a federation of unions that organized workers in industrial unions in the United States and Canada from 1935 to 1955.
explicit_text: I was part of the CIO union, and it made a big difference in how we worked and fought for our rights. Some bosses tried to put me and another girl on the worst jobs, like the vinegar job, hoping we'd quit, but we stuck it out because we had our union cards. The colored girls especially jumped at the chance to join the CIO and fought hard once they got involved. Even some of the most religious Polish women became fierce union fighters once they understood the union's power. The CIO gave us a voice against unfair treatment and helped us stand up to bosses who tried to push us around.

entity_description: movement to end slavery in the United States
entity_description_wiki: In the United States, abolitionism, the movement that sought to end slavery in the country, was active from the colonial era until the American Civil War, the end of which brought about the abolition of American slavery, except as punishment for a crime, through the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution (ratified 1865).
explicit_text: The slaves on our plantation were aware of the talk about Abolitionism before the war, and it caused them distress. They heard what we would now call propaganda, but they tried to comfort each other with songs expressing faith in God. Despite the tension, my father seemed confident they would be cared for, and the slaves remained mostly loyal during the conflict.

entity_description_wiki: Harlan County is located in southeastern Kentucky.
explicit_text: The Harlan County Library Literacy Project aimed to create six booklets about local people and issues to use as literacy materials for Appalachian students. We started the project after surveys showed students wanted stories reflecting their Appalachian culture and experiences. The first booklet, Hard Times, Happy Times, was very well received, and subsequent booklets were developed through interviews with locals from Harlan and Letcher Counties and members of the Virginia Black Lung Association. The project gained positive feedback, was distributed widely in Eastern Kentucky, and even earned the Kentucky Literacy Commission's Outstanding Program Award in 1992.

entity_description_wiki: John, Johnny, or Johnie Watson may refer to:
explicit_text: My great-great-great grandfather, John W. Watson, was a soldier in the Confederate States Army during the Civil War. He wrote a series of heartfelt letters to his wife, Margaret C. These letters have become treasured family artifacts that connect me deeply to my ancestors and their experiences during that turbulent time.

entity_description_wiki: Cambodia, officially the Kingdom of Cambodia, is a country in Mainland Southeast Asia.
explicit_text: I was born in Cambodia, Phnom Penh, in 1972 before the country collapsed under the communist takeover. My family and I suffered greatly during the Khmer Rouge regime, being evacuated from the city to the jungle where many people died from starvation and executions. After the communists collapsed in 1979, we faced continued hardship and eventually escaped Cambodia to seek refuge in Thailand. Cambodia is my homeland, and despite all the pain, it remains a deep part of who I am.

explicit_text: The Khao-I-Dang Refugee Camp in Thailand was where my family reunited after our dangerous escape from Cambodia. explicit_text: I grew up in the Umphiem Refugee Camp in Thailand, where my family lived for about 14 or 15 years. The camp was a place of safety for many Karen people like my family during difficult times.

entity_description: free-reed six-pipe bamboo mouthorgan of the Hmong people
explicit_text: The qeej is a traditional Hmong reed instrument that my father has played since he was five years old. He learned it from his dad and eventually bought his own at age 30 for 3,000 baht. The qeej is very important in our culture, played at funerals to honor the dead and during the Hmong New Year celebrations. It’s a difficult instrument to learn because it involves both musical and spoken parts. My father’s qeej traveled with us from the refugee camp in Thailand all the way to the United States, symbolizing our heritage and identity.

explicit_text: New York University was where my great-grandmother Adele Fiore studied law in 1920, at a time when few women were admitted to such programs. entity_description_wiki: Lefse (Norwegian pronunciation: [ˈlɛ́fsə̌], cognate to loaf), dialectally also lefsa (among others), is a traditional soft Norwegian flatbread, also found regionally in Sweden (Swedish: läfsa, löfsa).
explicit_text: Lefse is a cherished Norwegian food that my family has been making for generations, especially at Christmas. It’s a staple in our diet and even considered a 'first food' for babies in our community. My 95-year-old grandmother was still making lefse until recently, and it reminds me of my Norwegian heritage and family traditions. I’ve taken it upon myself to keep the tradition alive by teaching others about lefse and its significance.

The family lived comfortably there by 1935, owning a nice house and a luxury Packard car. New Jersey was also the site of the scrap metal business run by Joseph's sons after World War II, which was tied to the Italian Mafia. Joseph died in New Jersey in 1962, marking the end of his chapter in our family history.

explicit_text: The Americanization Movement was a big influence on my great-great-grandfather Joseph Grande's life as he grew up in New Jersey during the early 1900s. entity_description_wiki: Bhutan, officially the Kingdom of Bhutan, is a landlocked country in South Asia, located in the Eastern Himalayas, bordering China to the north and northwest and India to the south and southeast.
explicit_text: My parents and grandparents were born in Bhutan, a landlocked country in the Himalayas ruled by kings since 1907. Bhutan was considered our motherland, but the violence and discrimination made it impossible for us to stay.

explicit_text: In the fall of 1918, the Spanish flu pandemic swept over Canada and struck my great-grandfather Wilhelm, who was just a boy on a German Lutheran farm in Manitoba. He became seriously ill with double pneumonia and was given only twenty minutes to live by the town doctor. The pandemic was a pivotal event that shaped his future and, ultimately, my family's history.

Nicholas was a key figure in our family's migration story and their integration into American society.

explicit_text: After Pinedale, we were sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center, which was the largest of all the camps with about 10,000 people. I was impressed by how big it was, with many barracks, tall fences, guard towers, and the surrounding landmarks like Castle Rock and Abalone Mountain. Although it was intimidating, I was young and didn’t feel scared, just amazed. Many people from Auburn, including my family and friends, were there in the same ward. Sports like basketball and baseball were our main activities to keep busy.
implicit_text: After the initial assembly center, we were sent to the largest of all the camps with about 10,000 people. Sports like basketball and baseball were our main activities to keep busy.

entity_aliases: WW2|WWII|WW 2|WW II|World War Two|2nd World War|World War 2|the Second World War|Second World War|II World War
entity_description_wiki: World War II, or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945), was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.
explicit_text: World War II was a defining period for my family. The war separated us, with some siblings stuck in Japan and others in Hawaii. My father served in the Military Intelligence Service as a translator, while my uncle fought in the 100th Infantry Battalion. The war also brought hardship to my mother in Japan and led to tragic losses, like my uncle who died as part of the kamikaze unit in Okinawa. It was a time that shaped our values and sense of identity deeply.
implicit_text: The global conflict in the 1940s was a defining period for my family. The war separated us, with some siblings stuck in East Asia and others in the Pacific islands. My father served in the Military Intelligence Service as a translator, while my uncle fought in a famous infantry battalion composed mainly of Japanese Americans. The war also brought hardship to my mother in East Asia and led to tragic losses, like my uncle who died as part of a suicide attack unit in a southern island battle. It was a time that shaped our values and sense of identity deeply.
cues: family separation|Military Intelligence Service|100th Infantry Battalion|kamikaze unit|Okinawa|hardship|identity

entity_aliases: 100th Infantry Battalion (United States)|100th Infantry Battalion of the US|100th Infantry Battalion (US)
explicit_text: My uncle, the younger brother of my father, was part of the 100th Infantry Battalion during World War II. This was a unit composed mainly of Japanese Americans who wanted to prove their loyalty to the United States. While my father served in Military Intelligence, my uncle fought bravely in combat. Their service was a source of pride for our family and the Japanese American community.

explicit_text: An interesting connection in our family story involves a Japanese interpreter who helped my father locate his older sister in Japan after the war. This interpreter had been educated at New York University before returning to Japan during the war. It was a remarkable coincidence that someone with ties to NYU played a key role in reuniting family members separated by the conflict.

entity_description_wiki: Suntory Holdings Limited is a Japanese multinational brewing and distilling company.
explicit_text: Suntory was the whiskey company connected to my grandmother Chiyo Konishi’s family in Yokohama. Her father was a distributor for Suntory whiskey in that area, which meant the family was fairly well off. This connection to Suntory was part of the family’s background before they immigrated to the United States.

explicit_text: Amelia Street School was the public grammar school my mother and aunt attended in Little Tokyo, Los Angeles. At school, they were not allowed to speak Japanese and had to use English, which created a cultural divide since their father insisted on Japanese at home. My mother recalled the penny lunches there, where students could buy milk and dessert for just a few cents. The school was an important part of their childhood experience growing up in the Japanese American community.

entity_aliases: Tule Lake War Relocation Center|Tule Lake Relocation Center|TULE
entity_description_wiki: The Tule Lake War Relocation Center, also known as the Tule Lake Segregation Center, was an American concentration camp located in Modoc and Siskiyou counties in California and constructed in 1942 by the United States government to incarcerate Japanese Americans, forcibly removing them from their homes on the West Coast of the United States.
explicit_text: After Pinedale, we were sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center, which was a huge, dusty camp. I spent about a year there during my teenage years. It was tough but I managed to find some normalcy with friends and even got a waitress job using a relative's name to earn spending money. Tule Lake was also where my father made the decision to pledge loyalty to the U.S. rather than return to Japan.
implicit_text: After the assembly center, we were sent to a huge, dusty camp in northern California. I spent about a year there during my teenage years. It was tough but I managed to find some normalcy with friends and even got a waitress job using a relative's name to earn spending money. That camp was also where my father made the decision to pledge loyalty to the U.S. rather than return to Japan.

explicit_text: My grandfather was taken by the FBI to a camp in Missoula, Montana after Pearl Harbor. I heard from an Italian man who was there that they had a mock hearing. It was a harsh experience, and my grandfather was later transferred to other camps in New Mexico before being released after about a year.

entity_aliases: WW2|WWII|WW 2|WW II|World War Two|2nd World War|World War 2|the Second World War|Second World War|II World War
entity_description_wiki: World War II, or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945), was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.
explicit_text: During World War II, Japanese Americans were subjected to harsh treatment and internment, which was not widely known or understood at the time. I remember how people didn’t protest much because we were a small, quiet community, and culturally we don’t speak out. The war years were filled with fear, suspicion, and isolation, especially after Pearl Harbor. My family’s experience was shaped by this larger conflict, which affected all aspects of our lives.

entity_aliases: Tule Lake War Relocation Center|Tule Lake Relocation Center|TULE
entity_description_wiki: The Tule Lake War Relocation Center, also known as the Tule Lake Segregation Center, was an American concentration camp located in Modoc and Siskiyou counties in California and constructed in 1942 by the United States government to incarcerate Japanese Americans, forcibly removing them from their homes on the West Coast of the United States.
explicit_text: Tule Lake was known as a camp where some radical groups among the internees were held, and I remember seeing pictures of people exercising in military formation there. There were some who resisted and organized within the camp, which made it stand out from others. My father and his friend Bob were taught military style by Kibei, which connected to what happened at Tule Lake. Although I don’t know much about the camp itself, it had a reputation for housing the more defiant internees.

explicit_text: I was accepted to the engineering school at UC Berkeley but ultimately decided not to attend. Instead, I went to UCLA and later joined the army during the Korean War. Berkeley was part of my early academic options, but my path changed due to the war and my evolving interests. It was a significant choice point in my life.

explicit_text: Hiroshima is where my family originated, and it was also the city devastated by the atomic bomb during World War II. I was offered a job there with the Atomic Bomb Casualty Commission, which would have taken me back to my ancestral home. However, family traditions prevented me from taking the job and moving there with my wife. Hiroshima remains a significant place in my family's history and identity.

explicit_text: Bob Fujimoto was the owner of the gas station where I worked when I first met my wife. We didn't know each other before that, but I was pumping gas for her one day and that's how we connected. Bob was not related to us, but his gas station on Sawtelle was the place where our paths crossed. It was a simple moment that led to a lifelong relationship.

entity_description_wiki: Portland ( PORT-lənd) is the most populous city in the U.S. state of Oregon.
explicit_text: I grew up in Portland, Oregon, attending Elliot Elementary School until the third grade. Most Japanese Americans lived on the west side of the Willamette River, but my family was one of the few Japanese families in our neighborhood. My great uncle and aunt ran a small grocery store there, competing with Safeway. After the war, I stayed in Portland for a year while my dad trained in Los Angeles.
implicit_text: I grew up in a major city in the Pacific Northwest, attending a local elementary school until the third grade. Most Japanese Americans lived on the west side of the river that runs through the city, but my family was one of the few Japanese families in our neighborhood. My great uncle and aunt ran a small grocery store there, competing with a large national supermarket chain. After the war, I stayed in that city for a year while my dad trained in Southern California.

entity_description: 1942 U.S. presidential order for the internment of Japanese-Americans
entity_description_wiki: Executive Order 9066 was a United States presidential executive order signed and issued during World War II by United States president Franklin D. Roosevelt on February 19, 1942.
explicit_text: After Pearl Harbor, Executive Order 9066 was issued, and I remember reading the instructions about where we had to report and what we could bring. It was shocking to be told that all Japanese ancestry people, regardless of citizenship, had to comply with these orders. Our family began preparing to leave, trying to sell or get rid of belongings, not knowing what the future held.

explicit_text: The Scottsboro Boys case was a significant rallying point for labor and civil rights activists during the 1930s. We held mass rallies on the waterfront at noon hour to raise awareness and support for their cause. The case symbolized the broader struggle against injustice and was often linked with other labor and political campaigns like those for Tom Mooney and the Kentucky miners. It was part of the issues I spoke about when addressing workers and organizing public support.
implicit_text: The case involving nine young African American men falsely accused in the 1930s was a significant rallying point for labor and civil rights activists. We held mass rallies on the waterfront at noon hour to raise awareness and support for their cause. The case symbolized the broader struggle against injustice and was often linked with other labor and political campaigns like those for a famous labor activist and the Kentucky miners. It was part of the issues I spoke about when addressing workers and organizing public support.

explicit_text: Nate Branch was one of the notable speakers on the San Francisco waterfront during the labor struggles of the 1930s. Alongside Jim Branch and Sam Darcy, he was known for his effective speaking style that helped rally workers. I remember attending meetings where he spoke, and his contributions were valuable to the movement. These men brought different styles but shared a commitment to advancing workers' rights and Communist Party goals.

These men brought different styles but shared a commitment to advancing workers' rights and Communist Party goals.

explicit_text: California Hall in San Francisco was a key venue for mass meetings held by the Communist Party before the 1934 strike. We used to pack the hall for rallies where speakers like Sam Darcy would address hundreds of workers and supporters. The hall was near City Hall and served as an important gathering place for political and labor events. Interestingly, before the strike, the Nazi Party had its headquarters there, which added a tense political backdrop to our activities.

entity_description_wiki: Haight-Ashbury () is a district of San Francisco, California, named for the intersection of Haight and Ashbury streets.
explicit_text: After the camps, my mother found a place for us to live in the Haight-Ashbury neighborhood of San Francisco. A friend of hers owned the flat and we rented the bottom unit. It was one of the first places we stayed as we tried to rebuild our lives after the war. This neighborhood was part of our early post-internment experience.

entity_description_wiki: Japantown (Japanese: 日本町, Hepburn: Nihonmachi), also known historically as Japanese Town, is a neighborhood in the Western Addition district of San Francisco, California.
explicit_text: Eventually, we moved to a flat right outside of Japantown on Pine Street in San Francisco. Japantown was a central place for the Japanese American community and where I lived when I got married. It was a place where many young people from the camps connected and rebuilt their social lives. Japantown became a symbol of resilience for us after the war.

explicit_text: My family attended Pine United Methodist Church in San Francisco, which played an important role in my life. It was through this church and the influence of Reverend Lloyd Wake that I met my husband. The church became a gathering place for many young Japanese Americans coming out of the camps, serving as both a spiritual and social center.

explicit_text: Art Emi was my younger brother, and like the rest of us, he experienced the upheaval of the war and relocation. Though not much was said about him specifically, he was part of our large family that stayed together through the camps. Our parents worked hard to keep us all united, and Art was part of that close-knit family dynamic. We all shared the struggles and memories of those years.

explicit_text: The loyalty questionnaire at Heart Mountain was a tense and confusing time for all of us. Questions 27 and 28 were especially controversial, asking about our willingness to serve in the military and forswear allegiance to Japan. My brother Kazuto and Frank Emi were among the first to say no, and many others followed. It caused divisions and tough choices, but I mostly went along with my brother because I was unsure. It was a defining moment in our camp experience.

entity_description_wiki: Tagami may refer to:
explicit_text: Tagami was one of General MacArthur’s chief advisors during the Occupation of Japan. He was a Kibei Nisei, meaning he was born in the U.S. but educated in Japan, and he was an excellent person to have on our side. Tagami helped guide the occupation policies and was respected by both the American forces and the Japanese people. His presence made a big difference in how the occupation was conducted.
implicit_text: The chief advisor to the supreme commander during the post-war occupation was a second-generation Japanese American who was born in the U.S. but educated in Japan. He was an excellent person to have on our side. He helped guide the occupation policies and was respected by both the American forces and the Japanese people. His presence made a big difference in how the occupation was conducted.

explicit_text: My father, Hideo Inaba, immigrated to the United States from Ibaraki Prefecture near Tokyo when he was about ten years old. He worked hard to get through dental school because he wanted a career easier than the farming life his parents had. He opened his own dental practice in Riverside, California, and was well respected in the community. During World War II, he was arrested by the FBI and sent to internment camps, but before that, he generously volunteered free dental care to the poor through church referrals. After the war, he was fortunate to return to his intact dental office thanks to the kindness of his landlady.

entity_description: ethnic group; Americans of Japanese ancestry
explicit_text: Our Japanese American community in Riverside was quite small and a minority among other groups. My father supported the community by donating to the Japanese community church and volunteering free dental care for anyone in need, regardless of their background. Despite the challenges during the war, including forced removal and internment, the community tried to stay connected and support each other. After the war, friendships with other minority groups, like Mexican American kids at school, helped us feel some solidarity.

explicit_text: I lived in the Children's Village at Manzanar as an infant after my mother gave me up for adoption during the war. The care there was excellent, thanks to Mrs. Matsumoto and the dedicated volunteers, and notably, no children died in the camp. My legal adoption was processed through the Manzanar social worker, Ms. Robbins, in 1946.

entity_description: historical kingdom in parts of present-day Japan
entity_description_wiki: The Ryukyu Kingdom was a kingdom in the Ryukyu Islands from 1429 until 1872, when it was reorganized as the Ryukyu Domain, before being fully abolished in 1879.
explicit_text: The Ryukyu Kingdom was the original political entity in Okinawa before it became a Japanese prefecture. It was ruled by kings from the Sho family after unification of the three tribes. explicit_text: During my time at Tule Lake War Relocation Center, I washed dishes and practiced shamisen. I regret not learning English there, but I did my best to keep busy. Life in the camp was hard, but some people even missed it because food was secured for families with many children.

explicit_text: Port Arthur was a heavily fortified Russian port that Japan captured during the Russo-Japanese War in 1905. This victory was significant and caused fear among Americans, especially government officials and the press. The fall of Port Arthur symbolized Japan's military strength and influenced how Japanese immigrants were viewed in the United States.

entity_description_wiki: The soroban (算盤, そろばん; counting tray) is an abacus developed in Japan.
explicit_text: I was very good at soroban, the traditional abacus, and used to stand in front of the class calling out numbers for addition while calculating on my soroban. It was my best subject in school, and I can still do it faster than machines when I go to stores. My older and younger brothers were also good at it, and my younger brother still writes to me with very neat handwriting even now. Soroban was a skill I took pride in during my school days.

entity_description_wiki: World War II, or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945), was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.
explicit_text: World War II deeply affected my family and community. I was born in 1924 and was a junior in high school when the war started, right after Pearl Harbor. The war brought fear and uncertainty, especially for Japanese Americans like us, who faced forced relocation and internment. My parents were stoic and strong throughout, never showing panic despite the hardships we endured during this time.

entity_aliases: Executive Order of February 19, 1942|Executive Order No. 9066|EO 9066|United States Executive Order 9066
entity_description: 1942 U.S. presidential order for the internment of Japanese-Americans
entity_description_wiki: Executive Order 9066 was a United States presidential executive order signed and issued during World War II by United States president Franklin D. Roosevelt on February 19, 1942.
explicit_text: Executive Order 9066 was the government order that forced over 120,000 Japanese Americans, including my family, to leave our homes with only two or three weeks' notice. We had to sell our belongings quickly, often at a fraction of their value. This order uprooted our lives and sent us to camps far from California, changing everything for us.

entity_aliases: Tule Lake War Relocation Center|Tule Lake Relocation Center|TULE
entity_description_wiki: The Tule Lake War Relocation Center, also known as the Tule Lake Segregation Center, was an American concentration camp located in Modoc and Siskiyou counties in California and constructed in 1942 by the United States government to incarcerate Japanese Americans, forcibly removing them from their homes on the West Coast of the United States.
explicit_text: I was one of the first to be sent to Tule Lake War Relocation Center, where I was assigned to Block 415 C. Tule Lake later became the segregated camp for those who answered 'no' on the loyalty questionnaire. It was a large camp with about 20,000 people, and life there was organized into blocks and wards, trying to mimic a small town.

explicit_text: World War II dramatically affected my family’s life. We returned to Japan in late 1940, and the war started officially on December 8, 1941, the day I began school there. The war brought suspicion on us from both the FBI in the U.S. and the Japanese secret police. My father was involved in sending care packages to Japanese troops during the earlier conflicts, but the war made life very difficult. It was a time of fear, surveillance, and uncertainty for our family.

entity_description_wiki: Tatsuta Maru (龍田丸), was a Japanese  ocean liner owned by Nippon Yusen Kaisha (NYK).
explicit_text: The Tatsuta Maru was the ship that took my family from the United States to Japan in November 1940. It was a 14-day journey, and we landed in Yokohama. Our ship was the last to leave San Francisco before the war started, as the next ship had to turn back. This voyage marked a major turning point for us, as we left behind our life in America and returned to Japan on the eve of World War II.

explicit_text: I was actively involved in the 1971 ILWU strike, serving full picket duty at Berth 146 in Wilmington. The bosses, including Local 94 leaders like Jim North, even brought us food trucks, and most of us managed financially during the strike thanks to savings and my wife's work.

explicit_text: I grew up in Lynch, Kentucky, which is located in the northeast part of Harlan County. Lynch was built by U.S. Steel starting in 1917, and it was a coal mining town with a strong sense of community. The town had segregated but equal schools and housing for Black and white residents, and the union helped maintain harmony among workers. I attended the Lynch Colored Public School, which was owned by U.S. Steel, and lived in one of the camps where people of different races and ethnicities lived together within their community.

explicit_text: Lynch, Kentucky, was the U.S. Steel coal camp in the Tri-City Area where I grew up near Benham. It was founded after International Harvester sold land to U.S. Steel, and it had a slightly different culture and rivalries with Benham. Lynch did not have a credit union until the 1960s, unlike Benham, which had one sponsored by International Harvester since the 1930s. People from Lynch and Benham rarely dated outside their communities due to these rivalries.

explicit_text: The Progressive Mine Workers were a rival labor union to the United Mine Workers of America during the violent union wars in Harlan County, Kentucky, known as Bloody Harlan. They were active in Benham, where I was born, and their conflicts with the UMWA led to shootings and armed standoffs in the town. This rivalry was a defining part of the coal mining labor struggles in the 1930s and 1940s.

This event connects my family directly to the early frontier conflicts in Kentucky and the struggles of settlers in that era.

The event brought the community together, with crowds sometimes over 100,000 people, creating a sense of freedom and excitement for us kids.

explicit_text: 250Lex was a year-long celebration we organized to mark Lexington’s 250th anniversary, starting planning about two years ago. Unlike other southern cities that had only a weekend celebration, we chose a full year to highlight different themes each month, like Music Month, Culinary Month, and Sports Month. It was a huge collaborative effort involving a commission, co-chairs, and ambassadors who helped plan and execute events that engaged thousands of people. This celebration allowed us to recognize both the good and complex parts of our history while looking forward to the future.

entity_description_wiki: Ancient Greek (Ἑλληνική, Hellēnikḗ [hellɛːnikɛ́ː]) includes the forms of the Greek language used in ancient Greece and the ancient world from around 1500 BC to 300 BC. It is often roughly divided into the following periods: Mycenaean Greek (c. 1400 – c. 1200 BC), Dark Ages (c. 1200 – c. 800 BC), the Archaic or Homeric period (c. 800 – c. 500 BC), and the Classical period (c. 500 – c. 300 BC).
explicit_text: I majored in classical Greek at the University of Michigan because I loved the language, even though it was not practical. It was part of my academic passion before moving into library science.

The rural nature of Knott County shaped the school community and the lives of the students who stayed there during the week.

explicit_text: After Benham’s school closed around 1961 or 1962, I transferred to Lynch, Kentucky, for better quality education. Lynch was established by U.S. Steel Corporation and had a diverse population including many European immigrants and African Americans from the South. The cultural mix was very different from Benham, and I enjoyed my time there, especially in subjects like mathematics and chemistry. Lynch also had a strong football culture where I learned valuable lessons about teamwork and communication.

entity_description: city in Harlan County, Kentucky, United States of America
entity_description_wiki: Lynch is a home rule-class city in Harlan County, Kentucky, United States.
explicit_text: Lynch, Kentucky, is where I grew up and where much of my family’s coal mining history took place. Back in the 1950s and 60s, it was a bustling coal town with several camps, but when Portal 31 closed in the early 60s, many families had to move away. My father worked at Portal 31 and later Portal 32, and my grandfather was a brakeman there too. Even after the mines closed, I stayed in Lynch while working for U.S. Steel and later the Department of Mines and Minerals. Lynch holds a deep place in my heart as the center of my family’s coal mining legacy.
implicit_text: The coal town where I grew up is where much of my family’s mining history took place. My father worked at that mine and later another nearby, and my grandfather was a brakeman there too. Even after the mines closed, I stayed in the town while working for a major steel company and later the state mining department. This town holds a deep place in my heart as the center of my family’s coal mining legacy.

entity_description: mine in Harlan County, Kentucky, United States of America
explicit_text: Portal 31 was a major coal mine in Lynch where my father, grandfather, and great-grandfather all worked. It was active during the 30s, 40s, and into the 60s before it closed in the early 60s, which led to a big layoff and many families leaving the area. My grandfather worked there as a brakeman and even experienced serious injury on the job. Today, Portal 31 has been reopened as a tourist site, preserving the history of those days. It’s a symbol of our family’s deep roots in coal mining.

It’s a reminder of how important local institutions were to coal families.

explicit_text: The McLain Family Band is really unique because we play bluegrass music deeply rooted in our region, but also enriched by a wide variety of influences. Our father, with his background in music theory from Denison University and Harvard, and folklore studies at UNC Chapel Hill, brought a broad musical perspective that shaped our sound. We grew up in Hindman, Kentucky, and played music that connected us to our community and to the traditions of the mountains. Bill Monroe was a close friend who appreciated our creativity and originality, which blended traditional bluegrass with other musical elements.

entity_description: city in Harlan County, Kentucky, United States of America

explicit_text: I was born in Lynch, Kentucky in 1949, and I grew up in a coal camp called Number Three there. Lynch was one of the Tri-Cities along with Cumberland and Benham, and it was home to the U.S. Steel Mine. I attended West Main School in Lynch, which was part of the Lynch Independent School District, and I have many memories of the close-knit community and the pool room where we socialized. My family lived in company housing, and when my father retired from mining, we had to move to Cumberland.

explicit_text: The Missouri Plan was proposed by the Alabama Constitutional Commission as a model for appointing judges, though not for their retention. entity_aliases: OASDI|Social Security (United States)|Social Security|Social Security in the United States
entity_description_wiki: In the United States, Social Security is the commonly used term for the federal Old-Age, Survivors, and Disability Insurance (OASDI) program and is administered by the Social Security Administration (SSA).
explicit_text: I remember when my father got his first Social Security check, which was about twenty-one dollars. He was proud because he felt he had earned it by paying into the system during his working years. Over time, his monthly benefit increased to nearly forty dollars by the time he died, providing him a reliable income he never had before. As governor, I expanded Social Security coverage to many public employees in Arkansas, including county officials, city workers, school staff, and teachers, ensuring they had retirement benefits. Social Security was a cornerstone of the social programs that helped provide security for working people and retirees.

entity_description: United States federal government public work relief program part of the New Deal
entity_description_wiki: The Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) was a government work relief program that ran from 1933 to 1942 in the United States for unemployed, unmarried men ages 18–25 and eventually expanded to ages 17–28, who volunteered amid widespread unemployment.
explicit_text: The Civilian Conservation Corps, or CCC, was one of the New Deal work programs I greatly respected. It was part of Roosevelt's efforts to provide jobs where people had to earn their benefits through work. Programs like the CCC helped many during tough economic times by giving them meaningful employment rather than just welfare. I supported these kinds of initiatives because they aligned with my belief that able-bodied people should work for what they receive.

entity_description: 1930s New Deal program for youth in the United States
entity_description_wiki: The National Youth Administration (NYA) was a New Deal agency sponsored by Franklin D. Roosevelt during his presidency.
explicit_text: The National Youth Administration was another important New Deal program I admired, designed to provide work and education for young people during the Depression. Like the CCC and WPA, the NYA required participants to contribute effort in exchange for assistance. These programs reflected a philosophy I share—that aid should be tied to work or learning, not just given freely. The NYA helped prepare young people for the workforce and was part of the broader social safety net I supported.

explicit_text: Ted Kennedy was a prominent national Democratic figure in the 1970s, but I believe it would be very hard to sell him in Arkansas. The state's voters are independent and moderate, and Kennedy's liberal reputation doesn't resonate well here. The state Democratic Party needs to find a voice that reflects moderation and middle America rather than liberalism.

entity_description: Settlement of the 1876 U.S. presidential election
entity_description_wiki: The Compromise of 1877, also known as the Wormley Agreement, the Tilden-Hayes Compromise, the Bargain of 1877, or the Corrupt Bargain, was an informal political deal in the United States that settled the intense dispute over the results of the 1876 presidential election, in which Republican candidate Rutherford B. Hayes was declared the winner.
explicit_text: The Tilden–Hayes compromise of 1876 was a pivotal moment when Rutherford B. Hayes became president by agreeing to remove federal troops from the South. This decision effectively ended Reconstruction and allowed white landowners to regain control, reversing many gains made by black Americans. It set the stage for segregation and disenfranchisement in Mississippi and across the South.

At the time, some thought Rockefeller had almost no chance of winning the nomination, which was evident before the delegates even left their homes. This division within the party influenced how we approached the 1964 election in Mississippi, where the conservative Goldwater faction dominated.

explicit_text: My great-grandfather, Thomas S. Dabney, was a significant figure in my family history. He originally lived in Gloucester County, Virginia, but moved to Mississippi in 1835 when his tobacco lands were depleted. There, he established a successful cotton plantation in Hinds County and once cleared $50,000 in a year, which was a considerable sum at the time. Unfortunately, he was ruined during the Civil War and lost everything. His life was also the subject of a book written by Susan Dabney Smedes, his daughter.

entity_description_wiki: Garland County is a county located in the U.S. state of Arkansas.
explicit_text: In 1946, Garland County was dominated by the McLaughlin machine, which controlled illegal gambling and manipulated law enforcement and elections. I was involved in leading a G.I. revolt there to challenge this entrenched corruption. Despite the machine's coercion and violence, including assassinations and foreclosure tactics, we organized armed groups to monitor elections and reduce fraud. My election from this county marked the start of a broader political fight against the corrupt system.

entity_description: 1947 anti-segregation nonviolent protest in the southern United States
entity_description_wiki: The Journey of Reconciliation, also called "First Freedom Ride", was a form of nonviolent direct action to challenge state segregation laws on interstate buses in the Southern United States.
explicit_text: In April 1947, I was part of the Journey of Reconciliation, which was the first freedom ride into the South. This was organized to test the Supreme Court's 1946 Irene Morgan decision that outlawed segregation on interstate buses. We traveled from Washington, D.C., through Richmond, North Carolina, Kentucky, Tennessee, and Virginia, deliberately sitting in mixed-race pairs to challenge Jim Crow laws. It was a tense and courageous effort involving about twenty-five of us, including notable figures like Bayard Rustin and Conrad Lynn.

explicit_text: My doctoral dissertation was a comparative analysis of the Articles of Confederation and the Constitution, which I published as a series of law review articles. I didn't intend to provoke controversy, but my academic work ended up conflicting with the prevailing political views. This was part of the broader context of my teaching and writing that put me at odds with the Citizens' Councils and other segregationist elements.

entity_description: outside the mainstream of legal theory
entity_description_wiki: Interposition is a claimed right of a U.S. state to oppose actions of the federal government that the state deems unconstitutional.
explicit_text: Interposition was a doctrine revived in the 1950s by Kilpatrick's book, which I reviewed critically because it was a long-discredited idea used to justify resistance to desegregation. Several southern states passed interposition resolutions based on this doctrine, which I found utterly preposterous. My review of Kilpatrick's book drew the attention and ire of the Citizens' Councils, as it challenged their constitutional arguments. I saw it as my duty to counteract this erroneous propaganda with what I believed to be the truth about constitutional law.

The marina later added fencing to prevent such accidents, and it remains a poignant place in our family history.

My usual half-hour commute turned into a three-hour crawl through local streets in Jersey City and Newark, surrounded by other evacuees and heavy police presence. It felt like a mass urban evacuation from a disaster movie.

explicit_text: After ensuring people at Federal Hall were cared for, I went to Ground Zero to assist firemen, police officers, and first aid workers. We helped move supplies and provided transportation for those who needed it, doing whatever was necessary to support the rescue and recovery efforts. It was a somber and intense place, but everyone worked together to get the job done.

entity_description: 1979 utility transport helicopter family by Bell
entity_description_wiki: The Bell 412 is a utility helicopter of the Huey family manufactured by Bell Helicopter.
explicit_text: Eagle One and Eagle Two, the helicopters we used on September 11, 2001, are both Bell 412 models. These helicopters are large and sit on skids, with rotor blades high enough to avoid contact with people, though the tail rotor remains a hazard. On that day, Eagle Two was outfitted with a mass casualty kit to transport up to four injured patients instead of the usual two. The Bell 412s were vital in quickly evacuating injured personnel from the Pentagon crash site to medical facilities.

explicit_text: I was aboard Eagle Two on September 11, 2001, which was the second helicopter to respond to the Pentagon attack. Our crew included two pilots, myself and the unit commander, plus two medical personnel from the Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences. We landed near the Pentagon and helped evacuate severely burned patients to the Washington Hospital Center. Eagle Two also returned to the area later to assist with surveillance missions for the FBI and Department of Defense.

explicit_text: I was working in the flower garden at Arlington House on the morning of September 11, 2001. The place became a vantage point as the FBI set up a command station nearby to oversee the situation.

entity_description_wiki: World War II, or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945), was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.
explicit_text: My father was a World War II veteran who landed at Omaha Beach on D-Day. He didn’t talk much about his experiences, but I learned a lot from my mom and from Time Life books on World War II that I got after I married. My dad would sometimes point out his unit insignia in photos and tell me about being in Luxembourg and Germany. His service shaped some of my views on military duty.

My dad would sometimes point out his unit insignia in photos and tell me about being in Luxembourg and Germany. His service shaped some of my views on military duty.

explicit_text: At Fort Knox, I went through reconnaissance scout training, which was very demanding and covered a wide range of skills like camouflage, explosives, map reading, and artillery adjustment. The training emphasized stealth and intelligence gathering rather than direct fighting. We had to complete a tough final test involving a timed ten-mile run with various tasks. This training prepared me for the complex missions I would face in Vietnam.

entity_description: World War II entertainment venue for military personnel
entity_description_wiki: The Hollywood Canteen operated at 1451 North Cahuenga Boulevard in the Los Angeles, California, neighborhood of Hollywood between October 3, 1942, and November 22, 1945, as a club offering food, dancing, and entertainment for enlisted men and women, who were usually on their way overseas during World War II. Even though the majority of visitors were US servicemen, the canteen was open to allied countries as well as women in all branches of service.
explicit_text: While stationed at Camp Pendleton, my friend Otto and I went to the Hollywood Canteen during liberty. There we saw celebrities like Mickey Rooney and Maria Montez. I even got to dance briefly with Maria Montez, which was a memorable experience amidst the war.

entity_description: region spanning the border south in Texas, United Staters

explicit_text: The Lower Rio Grande Valley, including Brownsville and McAllen, is where I grew up and experienced a very homogeneous culture. This region has remained one of the least affected by racial and ethnic diversity in the United States. My time in the Marine Corps was my first real exposure to the broader diversity of the country, which was eye-opening compared to the Valley. It’s a unique place with a strong Latino influence.

explicit_text: The A Shau Valley was a jungle, mountainous region in northern South Vietnam near the Laotian border where I was assigned during my second tour. It was a strategically important area, located in the middle of the Ho Chi Minh trail and heavily contested. Our Special Forces team, A102, was stationed there, and the valley was the site of intense combat and infiltration by North Vietnamese forces.

entity_description: 1945 major battle in which US Forces captured the island of Iwo Jima from Japan during World War II
entity_description_wiki: The Battle of Iwo Jima (硫黄島の戦い, Iōtō no Tatakai, Iōjima no Tatakai; 19 February – 26 March 1945) was a major battle in which the United States Marine Corps (USMC) and United States Navy (USN) landed on and eventually captured the island of Iwo Jima from the Imperial Japanese Army (IJA) during World War II. The American invasion, designated Operation Detachment, had the goal of capturing the island with its two airfields: South Field and Central Field.
explicit_text: I noticed that the World War II Memorial had posts for states and territories but lacked specific references to battles like Iwo Jima. Malaria was a big problem there; almost all of us contracted it and had to take Atabrine throughout our time overseas.

entity_description_wiki: The Imperial Valley (Spanish: Valle de Imperial or Valle Imperial) of Southern California lies in Imperial and Riverside counties, with an urban area centered on the city of El Centro.
explicit_text: I worked in the Imperial Valley where the temperature reached 115 degrees and the wind felt like fire. We grew cantaloupes by contract on a 40-acre ranch, working long hours sometimes until 10 p.m. The roads were unpaved and dusty, and transportation was by horseback. It was tough work, but I earned about $10 a day and persevered.

entity_description: 1942 U.S. presidential order for the internment of Japanese-Americans
entity_description_wiki: Executive Order 9066 was a United States presidential executive order signed and issued during World War II by United States president Franklin D. Roosevelt on February 19, 1942.
explicit_text: When Executive Order 9066 was issued, my family had to prepare to leave our home and give up my dad’s business, which was very hard for him. My mom was busy sorting and trying to sell our belongings, but people often offered peanuts for our stuff or even broke our Japanese dish sets. We had to leave everything behind and go to camp, which was a terrible experience. It was a turning point that changed our lives forever.

explicit_text: I am Mike Pelletier, born in Old Town, Maine in 1873. I have lived here all my life, working mostly in the Great Works pulp mill since 1887 after finishing school. I have a large family with 13 children, many of whom work in various places like Berlin, New Hampshire and Pittsburgh. Despite my age, I keep my enthusiasm and enjoy life with my wife. I am proud of my French Canadian heritage and my deep roots in this community.

I am proud of my French Canadian heritage and my deep roots in this community.

entity_description: United States federal government public work relief program part of the New Deal
entity_description_wiki: The Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC) was a government work relief program that ran from 1933 to 1942 in the United States for unemployed, unmarried men ages 18–25 and eventually expanded to ages 17–28, who volunteered amid widespread unemployment.
explicit_text: One of my youngest boys works in a Civilian Conservation Corps camp, which is a new kind of work for our family. The CCC is a government program that provides jobs, and it’s interesting to see the younger generation involved in it. While I worked in the pulp mill all my life, my children have branched out into different fields, including the CCC. It shows how times are changing from my youth to theirs.

explicit_text: Father Trudel was the priest who arrived in Old Town after the church was moved here from Great Works in 1870. He built the addition on the church and christened me, which makes him a significant figure in my life. He was one of the early priests serving multiple parishes, sometimes traveling far to minister. Father Trudel represents the spiritual leadership of our French Canadian Catholic community. His work helped shape the religious life of Old Town during my youth.

explicit_text: I am Kelsey L. Pharr, Miami's Negro undertaker and welfare worker. Born in Salisbury, North Carolina nearly fifty years ago, I have lived in Miami for twenty-five years, dedicating myself to serving my people. I started as a waiter and bellhop at the Royal Palm Hotel in 1914, then bought the undertaking business with partners and eventually took it over myself. I also organized a funeral benefit society to protect our community from exploitative insurance schemes. Beyond business, I have been deeply involved in church and youth work, especially with the A.M.E. Zion Church, striving to uplift and educate our people.

explicit_text: I am part of the Karen people, an ethnic group from Burma. My parents were born in Burma and escaped Burmese soldiers, which led our family to seek refuge in Thailand. Our community stayed strong through these difficult times until we resettled in the United States.

entity_description_wiki: World War II, or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945), was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.
explicit_text: During World War II, I served as a border policeman in Yugoslavia until the Nazis captured me in 1941. I was held as a prisoner of war in a labor camp in southern Germany until 1945. The war not only took me from my home but also led to my imprisonment and eventual displacement. It was the events of this war that ultimately led me to seek refuge and a new life in the United States.

entity_description: free-reed six-pipe bamboo mouthorgan of the Hmong people
explicit_text: The qeej is a bamboo flute that is central to Hmong culture, and I started playing it when I was 14 in Laos. It’s not just an instrument but a form of language and storytelling, used in spiritual ceremonies, funerals, entertainment, and personal enjoyment. After stopping during my time in the refugee camp and church involvement, I revived my playing when I came to the United States in 2000. I've been playing for almost 50 years and have learned from multiple teachers by listening and observing, preserving this important tradition.

entity_description_wiki: Cambodia, officially the Kingdom of Cambodia, is a country in Mainland Southeast Asia.
explicit_text: I was born in Cambodia, Phnom Penh, in 1972 before the country collapsed under communist rule. My family and I were evacuated from the city to the jungle during the Khmer Rouge regime, where many people died from starvation and violence. Despite losing nearly 200 family members, we survived by eating whatever we could find. Cambodia is my homeland, and even after all these years in the United States, I still miss it deeply.

explicit_text: After escaping Cambodia and reuniting with my family, we made a difficult journey to the Khao-I-Dang refugee camp in Thailand. The crossing was dangerous, involving crawling through barbed wire and avoiding guards who would shoot refugees. Khao-I-Dang was where we stayed before being accepted for resettlement in the United States. It was a place of both suffering and survival for many Cambodians like me.

explicit_text: I grew up in the Umphiem Refugee Camp in Thailand after my family moved there when I was just three months old. Life in the camp was hard because we couldn’t go outside due to Thai security restrictions, but I went to school there and made many childhood friends. My family stayed in the camp for about 14 or 15 years until we resettled in the United States in 2013. Leaving the camp was very emotional for me because I had to say goodbye to friends and family who still live there.

explicit_text: My parents and grandparents were born in Bhutan, a landlocked Himalayan kingdom ruled by kings since 1907. explicit_text: World War II had a profound impact on my early life even though I was born just after it ended. The war's shadow was always present in my childhood, shaping the environment and the community around me.

The union fostered a strong sense of camaraderie and family among its members, and I was proud to serve in leadership roles within ILWU Locals 8 and 40. The ILWU also played a key role during significant strikes and labor negotiations, shaping the waterfront's working conditions over the decades.

explicit_text: The 1948 West Coast waterfront strike was a defining event for my family and me. My father was a longshoreman working nights during that time, and I was about 11 or 12 years old. I remember the struggle as benefits were scarce and we had to rely on community support, like food baskets from our church. Though I hid in my room when the charity came, I now feel proud of how the strike and the waterfront shaped our family’s resilience and future.

entity_description_wiki: Chuuk Lagoon, previously Truk Atoll, is an atoll in the central Pacific Ocean.
explicit_text: There was a rumor among us that our invasion force was headed for Truk Lagoon, a heavily fortified Japanese base north of the Marshall Islands. Truk had been bombed extensively before, and a battle there would have been very bloody. However, the high command decided instead to attack right into the center of the Marshall Islands, bypassing Truk. This decision was controversial even in Washington because of the risk of being cut off.

explicit_text: Namur Island was where we landed during the Kwajalein Atoll assault in January 1944. We knew exactly where to land and what our objectives were, thanks to detailed briefings and a mock-up of the island. The island was heavily defended with pillboxes and blockhouses, and the fighting was fierce. I was in a hole near a crater when a massive explosion devastated E Company, causing about eighty percent casualties. Despite the chaos, we fought through the night and the next day, even when surrounded by Japanese forces.

entity_description_wiki: The International Longshore and Warehouse Union (ILWU) is a labor union which primarily represents dock workers on the West Coast of the United States, Hawaii, and in British Columbia, Canada; on the East Coast, the dominant union is the International Longshoremen's Association.
explicit_text: The International Longshore and Warehouse Union, or ILWU, has been a central part of my life and work on the waterfront. I joined the union as a casual in 1959 and became a registered longshoreman in 1968. The ILWU fostered a strong sense of camaraderie among workers and provided protections and benefits that improved over time. I served in leadership roles within the union, including president of Locals 8 and 40, and participated actively in union conventions and politics. The union's history, including strikes and negotiations, shaped the waterfront and our families profoundly.

Italy's military laws and economic conditions influenced my father's decision to emigrate.

explicit_text: The Chinese people made up the majority of the labor force on the Central Pacific Railroad where my father worked. They were the main cooks and controlled the food supply, which gave them significant power over the other workers. My father became a cook himself, preparing Italian dishes once a week, which made him popular among the workers who were tired of Chinese food. Despite tensions, the Chinese workers were essential to the railroad's construction and had a strong presence on the job.

entity_description: 1945 major battle in which US Forces captured the island of Iwo Jima from Japan during World War II
entity_description_wiki: The Battle of Iwo Jima (硫黄島の戦い, Iōtō no Tatakai, Iōjima no Tatakai; 19 February – 26 March 1945) was a major battle in which the United States Marine Corps (USMC) and United States Navy (USN) landed on and eventually captured the island of Iwo Jima from the Imperial Japanese Army (IJA) during World War II. The American invasion, designated Operation Detachment, had the goal of capturing the island with its two airfields: South Field and Central Field.
explicit_text: Iwo Jima was one of the major battles in the Pacific that my fellow servicemen talked about when I was in boot camp. The thought of carrying blood on an invasion like Iwo Jima was daunting, and it made me consider the realities of war. Although I was trained as a Quartermaster, I was aware of the intense fighting happening there.

explicit_text: I spent my entire two years of Navy service aboard the USS Roosevelt, an aircraft carrier based in Mayport, Florida. We were the first East Coast carrier to go to Vietnam, sailing around South Africa and stopping in places like Cuba and Brazil. entity_description_wiki: The Colmar Pocket (French: Poche de Colmar; German: Brückenkopf Elsass) was the area held in central Alsace, France, by the German Nineteenth Army from November 1944 to February 1945, against the U.S. 6th Army Group (6th AG) during World War II. It was formed when 6th AG liberated southern and northern Alsace and adjacent eastern Lorraine, but could not clear central Alsace.
explicit_text: My first real battle was in the Colmar Pocket near where France, Germany, and Switzerland meet, around January 1945. We faced heavy artillery and mortar fire, and I helped wounded men after an 88 mm shell hit a house where we were setting up a machine gun. The battle was intense, and I was promoted and awarded for my service there.
implicit_text: My first real battle was in a contested area near where France, Germany, and Switzerland meet, around January 1945. The battle was intense, and I was promoted and awarded for my service there.

explicit_text: I grew up in Madison, Wisconsin, after my family moved there in the late 1960s. We lived at Truax Field, which is now where the MATC parking lot is. entity_description_wiki: World War II, or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945), was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.
explicit_text: World War II was a terrifying time for me growing up in Prague under German occupation. We had to listen to shortwave radios secretly to hear news from the BBC, which was punishable by death. My grandfather was arrested by the Gestapo and died in Dachau concentration camp, and my aunt was sent to a forced labor camp. Those years aged me beyond my years and left lasting scars.
